title: Introduction to Durational Event Models
text: redeem package provides tools estimation Durational Event Models (DEM) via dem() function. framework extends standard Relational Event Models (REM), can fit using rem() function, explicitly modeling duration interactions. vignette provides theoretical overview model demonstrates application using simulated dataset.

title: Introduction to Durational Event Models
text: Unlike standard REMs treat events instantaneous, DEM framework characterizes interactions via two separate intensities: Incidence Process (\(\lambda^{0\rightarrow 1}\)): Models intensity pair actors \((,j)\) start interaction. Dissolution Process (\(\lambda^{1\rightarrow 0}\)): Models intensity pair actors \((,j)\) end already started interaction. intensities modeled : \[\lambda_{ij}^{0\rightarrow 1}(t, \beta, \alpha, \gamma^{0\rightarrow 1}) = \exp(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)^\top \beta + \alpha_i + \alpha_j + f(t, \gamma^{0\rightarrow 1}))\] \[\lambda_{ij}^{1\rightarrow 0}(t, \delta, \phi, \gamma^{1\rightarrow 0}) = \exp(u_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)^\top \delta + \phi_i + \phi_j + f(t, \gamma^{1\rightarrow 0}))\] : - \(\mathscr{H}_t\) defines history interactions time \(t\). - \(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)\) \(u_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)\) vectors summary statistics. - \(\alpha, \phi\) actor-specific popularity parameters. - \(f(t, \gamma) = \sum_{q=1}^Q \gamma_q \mathbb{}(c_{q-1} \le t < c_q)\) baseline step-function captures temporal variations data. indicator function \(\mathbb{}(c_{q-1} \le t < c_q)\) takes value 1 \(c_{q-1} \le t < c_q\) 0 otherwise, \(0 = c_0 < c_1 < \dots < c_Q\) specified change points \(\gamma = (\gamma_1, \dots, \gamma_Q)^\top\) baseline parameter vector (\(\gamma_1 = 0\) imposed model identifiability). Note look undirected interactions, parameters symmetric \(\) \(j\). model also implemented directed interactions allowing asymmetric parameters, .e., \(\phi_{,O}\) \(\phi_{,}\) outgoing effect actor \(\) dissolution intensity.

title: Introduction to Durational Event Models
text: package implements several key statistics capture network dynamics. full mathematical definitions descriptions available transformations (e.g., log, sig, bin), please refer redeem_terms documentation. Key terms include: Inertia: \(s_{ij}(\mathscr{H}_t) = N_{ij}(t)\), counting previous \(\j\) formation events. Degree Effects: Actor-specific activity (\(\alpha\)) popularity (\(\phi\)). Duration: dissolution process, elapsed time since beginning interaction. Common Partners: Shared outgoing (OSP) incoming (ISP) friends.

title: Introduction to Durational Event Models
text: estimation process can customized using function. function creates configuration object manages algorithmic behavior: estimate: Character; estimation method (“Blockwise” “NR”). “Blockwise” default recommended larger networks. it_max & tol: Integer Numeric; maximum number iterations convergence tolerance. can given vector length 2 specify different limits formation (first element) dissolution (second element) processes . accelerated: Logical; TRUE, uses SQUAREM acceleration MM updates speed convergence. Can also vector length 2 process-specific acceleration. verbose: Logical; TRUE, prints iteration-specific progress. simultaneous_interactions: Logical; controls whether actors can participate multiple interactions . return_data: Logical; whether include preprocessed data frames result. example, use iterations dissolution process:
code: ctrl <- control.redeem(it_max = c(50, 200), tol = c(1e-8, 1e-12))

title: Introduction to Relational Event Models
text: standard Relational Event Model (REM) conceptualizes social interactions instantaneous events continuous time. models stream interactions actors without associated durations (e.g., sending email, posting tweet, discrete behavioral action). generating mechanism REM multidimensional point process, potential directed dyad \((,j)\) distinct, continuous-time intensity (rate) interaction: \[\lambda_{ij}(\mathscr{H}_t, \beta, \alpha, \gamma) = \exp(s_{ij}(\mathscr{H}_t)^\top \beta + \alpha_i + \alpha_j + f(t, \gamma))\] : - \(\mathscr{H}_t\) history interactions time \(t\). - \(s_{ij}(\mathscr{H}_t)\) vector dynamic network statistics capturing structural history interactions time \(t\). - \(\beta\) structural parameters governing statistics. - \(\alpha_i\) \(\alpha_j\) actor-specific baseline activity popularity parameters (degree effects). - \(f(t, \gamma) = \sum_{q=1}^Q \gamma_q \mathbb{}(c_{q-1} \le t < c_q)\) baseline step-function captures temporal variations overall intensity. indicator function \(\mathbb{}(c_{q-1} \le t < c_q)\) takes value 1 \(c_{q-1} \le t < c_q\) 0 otherwise, \(0 = c_0 < c_1 < \dots < c_Q\) specified change points \(\gamma = (\gamma_1, \dots, \gamma_Q)^\top\) baseline parameter vector (\(\gamma_1 = 0\) imposed model identifiability).

title: Introduction to Relational Event Models

Degree Effects (Actor Heterogeneity): Including sender (\(\alpha_i\)) receiver (\(\alpha_j\)) degree effects important account inherent individual activity. Omitting degree effects often causes omitted variable bias structural parameter estimates artificially inflate absorb underlying actor heterogeneity. Temporal Effects: Including terms adapt baseline time accounts non-stationarity overall intensity. Overlooking temporal fluctuations assumes point occurrences identically sequenced exponential arrival gaps without burstiness varying base density.

title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: redeem package models intensities interaction formation dissolution using log-linear formulation. intensity (rate) dyad \((,j)\) time \(t\) : \[\lambda_{,j}(t) = \exp(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)^\top \beta + \alpha_i + \alpha_j + f(t, \gamma))\] : - \(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)\) vector sufficient statistics derived history interactions \(\mathscr{H}_t\) time \(t\). - \(\alpha_i, \alpha_j\) actor popularity parameters. - \(f(t, \gamma) = \sum_{q=1}^Q \gamma_q \mathbb{}(c_{q-1} \le t < c_q)\) baseline step-function representing temporal variation change points \(0 = c_0 < c_1 < \dots < c_Q\) (\(\gamma_1 = 0\)). vignette provides precise mathematical definitions sufficient network statistics implemented package, well guide adding custom statistics. complete list available formula terms documented redeem_terms reference manual page. model estimation, see help pages models via dem rem.

title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: sufficient statistic \(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)\) defined transformed count \(f(N(t))\), \(N(t)\) raw network statistic. package supports five standard transformations \(f(\cdot)\): achieve maximum performance likelihood estimation simulation, transformations computed incrementally --fly inside C++ state matrix. raw statistic increases value \(v\): Identity: \(f(s + v) = f(s) + v\). Log: \(f(s + v) = f(s) + \log(1 + v e^{-f(s)})\). Reciprocal: \(f(s + v) = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{f(s)} + v}\). Binary: \(f(s + v) = \mathbb{}(s + v > 0)\). Sigmoid: \(f(s + v) = \frac{K \cdot f(s) + v (1 - f(s))}{K + v (1 - f(s))}\).

title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: Triadic statistics capture structural closure. can calculated active edges (designated current_ DEM) historical event existence (designated general_ REM/DEM). Let \(\mathcal{}_t\) represent set interacting dyads (current_) previously interacted dyads (general_) time \(t\).

title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: Counts number third-party actors \(k\) sharing connection specified type \(\) \(j\): OSP (Outgoing Shared Partner): \(s_{,j}(t) = f(|\{k : (,k) \\mathcal{}_t \land (j,k) \\mathcal{}_t\}|)\) ISP (Incoming Shared Partner): \(s_{,j}(t) = f(|\{k : (k,) \\mathcal{}_t \land (k,j) \\mathcal{}_t\}|)\) OTP (Outgoing Two-Path): \(s_{,j}(t) = f(|\{k : (,k) \\mathcal{}_t \land (k,j) \\mathcal{}_t\}|)\) ITP (Incoming Two-Path): \(s_{,j}(t) = f(|\{k : (k,) \\mathcal{}_t \land (j,k) \\mathcal{}_t\}|)\)

title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: Count statistics identical degree statistics use total interaction counts (\(c\)) rather binary degrees (\(d\)): - -Count Sender: \(s_{,j}(t) = f(c_{, \text{}}(t))\) - -Count Receiver: \(s_{,j}(t) = f(c_{j, \text{}}(t))\) - -Count Sender: \(s_{,j}(t) = f(c_{, \text{}}(t))\) - -Count Receiver: \(s_{,j}(t) = f(c_{j, \text{}}(t))\) - Count Sum (undirected ): \(s_{,j}(t) = f(c_{}(t) + c_{j}(t))\) - Count Absolute Difference (undirected ): \(s_{,j}(t) = f(\|c_{}(t) - c_{j}(t)\|)\) degree count statistics can conveniently specified model formulas using degree() (degrees()) count() helper functions: -Degree / -Count Sender: degree(type = "out_sender") count(type = "out_sender") -Degree / -Count Receiver: degree(type = "out_receiver") count(type = "out_receiver") -Degree / -Count Sender: degree(type = "in_sender") count(type = "in_sender") -Degree / -Count Receiver: degree(type = "in_receiver") count(type = "in_receiver") Degree / Count Sum: degree(type = "sum") count(type = "sum") Degree / Count Absolute Difference: degree(type = "absdiff") count(type = "absdiff")

what: Developer Guide: Adding Custom Sufficient Statistics
title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: add new sufficient statistic (e.g., my_stat) package, follow two-step process:

previous_headings: Developer Guide: Adding Custom Sufficient Statistics
what: Step 1: Implement the Update Logic in C++
title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: Define C++ function src/ directory (e.g., new file src/sufficient_statistics.cpp) signature ValidateFunction. function computes change statistic state matrix event occurs:
code: #include "redeem/sufficient_statistics.h" #include "redeem/extension_api.hpp"  arma::uvec stat_my_stat(     Data_DEM &object,      arma::mat &data,      unsigned int &from,      unsigned int &to,      unsigned int &number_event,      unsigned int col_number,      std::string transformation,      unsigned int type ) {   if (from == 0 || to == 0) return arma::uvec();      // 1. Calculate which dyad indices are affected and the change value (val)   double val = (type == 1) ? 1.0 : -1.0;    arma::uvec affected_indices = { object.current_stats.find_from_to(from, to) };      // 2. Apply the update using the helper (handles transformations internally)   apply_update(object, affected_indices, col_number, val, transformation, data);      // 3. Return the indices of modified dyads   return affected_indices; }  // 4. Register the term in the global C++ Registry TERM_REGISTER("my_stat", stat_my_stat);

previous_headings: Developer Guide: Adding Custom Sufficient Statistics
what: Step 2: Write the R Initializer Function
title: Mathematical Definitions of Sufficient Statistics
text: R/init_terms.R, create initialization function named InitRedeemTerm.my_stat. function validates user-provided arguments sets initial values: steps completed, run devtools::document() update namespaces documentation, build package. new term now ready use dem() rem() specifying directly formula (e.g., ~ my_stat(transformation = "log")).
code: #' @keywords internal InitRedeemTerm.my_stat <- function(arglist, n_nodes, model_type, directed, ...) {   # Validate arguments against expected types and models   arglist <- check.RedeemTerm(arglist,     model_type = model_type, directed = directed,     expected = list(transformation = c("identity", "log", "recip", "bin", "sig"), K = "numeric"),     defaults = list(transformation = "identity", K = 1)   )      # Define initial statistic values at time 0 (optional)   eval_at_zero <- matrix(0, n_nodes, n_nodes)      # Return the configuration list   list(     base_name = "my_stat",     transformation = arglist$transformation,     eval_at_zero = eval_at_zero,     event_stream = arglist$event_stream   ) }

title: Relational Event and Durational Event Models
text: redeem R package estimation Durational Event Models (DEM) Relational Event Models (REM). features scalable block-coordinate ascent algorithm designed handle high-dimensional network data thousands actors time-varying effects.

title: Relational Event and Durational Event Models
text: Theory-Aligned: Implements framework described “Scalable Durational Event Models: Application Physical Digital Interactions” (arXiv:2504.00049). Decoupled intensities: Separately models incidence (formation) duration (dissolution) events. Scalable Estimation: Uses block-coordinate ascent algorithm (MM-based) efficiently estimate model. Flexible Baselines: Supports constant, piecewise-constant, semiparametric baselines. Support Constraints: Handles simultaneous interaction constraints (e.g., phone calls actors can one call time).

title: Relational Event and Durational Event Models
code: library(redeem)  # Example: n=50 nodes, directed events n_nodes <- 50  # events matrix: time, from, to, type (1=start, 0=end) events <- matrix(c(   1.0, 1, 5, 1,   2.5, 1, 5, 0,   3.2, 2, 10, 1,   4.8, 2, 10, 0 ), ncol = 4, byrow = TRUE) colnames(events) <- c("time", "from", "to", "type")  # Fit a Durational Event Model # Modeling both start (0->1) and end (1->0) transitions fit <- dem(   events = events,   n_nodes = n_nodes,   formula_0_1 = ~ current_interaction() + inertia(),   formula_1_0 = ~ duration(),   control = control.redeem(estimate = "Blockwise") )  # Summarize results summary(fit)

title: Durational Event Model (DEM) Estimation — dem
text: Durational Event Model (DEM) general framework analyzing durational events, extending standard Relational Event Models (REM) decoupling modeling event incidence event duration. characterizes dynamics via two separate continuous-time counting processes: Formation Process (\(0 \rightarrow 1\)) Counts number times   actor pair \((,j)\) starts interaction time \(t\). incidence intensity   denoted \(\lambda_{,j}^{0\rightarrow 1}(t | \mathscr{H}_t)\). Dissolution Process (\(1 \rightarrow 0\)) Counts number times   actor pair \((,j)\) stops interacting time \(t\). dissolution intensity   denoted \(\lambda_{,j}^{1\rightarrow 0}(t | \mathscr{H}_t)\). assumption processes non-homogeneous Poisson processes, intensities modeled : $$\lambda_{,j}^{0\rightarrow 1}(t | \mathscr{H}_t, \theta^{0\rightarrow 1}) = \exp(s_{,j}^{0\rightarrow 1}(\mathscr{H}_t)^\top \alpha^{0\rightarrow 1} + \beta_i^{0\rightarrow 1} + \beta_j^{0\rightarrow 1} + f(t, \gamma^{0\rightarrow 1}))$$ $$\lambda_{,j}^{1\rightarrow 0}(t | \mathscr{H}_t, \theta^{1\rightarrow 0}) = \exp(s_{,j}^{1\rightarrow 0}(\mathscr{H}_t)^\top \alpha^{1\rightarrow 0} + \beta_i^{1\rightarrow 0} + \beta_j^{1\rightarrow 0} + f(t, \gamma^{1\rightarrow 0}))$$ : \(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)\) vector dynamic network statistics     capturing history past interactions \(\mathscr{H}_t\). \(\alpha\) parameter vector determining covariate effects. \(\beta_i\) \(\beta_j\) actor-specific sociality/popularity     parameters (degree correction) capturing actor heterogeneity. \(f(t, \gamma)\) piecewise-constant step function modeling     temporal baseline fluctuations across set changepoints. satisfy Feller criterion ensure continuous-time counting process remains non-explosive, count-based network statistics (inertia common partners) typically log-transformed \(\log(x + 1)\) scale.

title: Durational Event Model (DEM) Estimation — dem
text: likelihood model separable respect \(\theta^{0\rightarrow 1}\) \(\theta^{1\rightarrow 0}\), allowing independent estimation incidence duration components. Traditional maximum likelihood estimation via standard Newton-Raphson requires computing inverting \(O(N^2)\) Hessian matrix, computationally prohibitive larger networks. bypass , redeem package implements highly scalable block-coordinate ascent algorithm separates parameter updates: Step 1: Update covariate parameters \(\alpha\) using     standard Newton-Raphson update. Step 2: Update high-dimensional actor popularity     baselines \(\beta\) using Minorization-Maximization     (MM) steps, avoiding explicit matrix inversion. Step 3: Update baseline step function parameters     \(\gamma\) via closed-form step. information provided Fritz et . (2026).

title: Durational Event Model (DEM) Estimation — dem
text: semiparametric = TRUE, baseline rates formation (\(0 \rightarrow 1\)) dissolution (\(1 \rightarrow 0\)) processes left completely unspecified. processes estimated separate Cox proportional hazards models using survival package. path: formation process, start event (1) treated failure,     inactive dyads time constitute risk set. dissolution process, end event (0) treated failure,     currently active interactions constitute risk set. exact waiting times (durations active inactive states)     conditioned , estimation based solely ordering events     relative dyadic intensities transition time. approach highly robust arbitrary temporal fluctuations baseline rates     since piecewise-constant temporal baselines changepoints need specified. Limitations: path support specialized     scalable estimation sender/receiver popularity effects (degree)     piecewise-constant temporal baselines.

title: MM Algorithm for Durational Event Models with Time-Varying Effects — estimate_mmt
text: algorithm decomposes log-likelihood updates blocks parameters sequentially. Specifically: Core effects (\(\beta\)) updated via Newton-Raphson. Degree effects (\(\alpha\)) updated using MM step         avoids explicit Hessian inversion high-dimensional actor sets. Temporal effects (\(\gamma\)) updated via similar MM step         across defined time changepoints.

Consequently, model correctly specified: empirical survival function residuals     closely match theoretical survival function standard     exponential distribution, \(S(r) = \exp(-r)\). title: redeem Model Terms — redeem_terms
text: help pages rem dem describe model formulation estimation details. page documents statistics available used model formulas, characterizing intensities event formation dissolution. redeem framework, models like DEM (fitted via dem) REM (fitted via rem) specified using R formulas. right-hand side formulas defines structural statistics covariates, term must specified separately explicit function call (e.g., ~ inertia() + reciprocity(window = 10)). terms support optional transformation argument \(f\). available transformations : "identity" (default): \(f(x) = x\) "log": \(f(x) = \log(x + 1)\) "recip": \(f(x) = 1/(x+1)\) "bin": \(f(x) = (x > 0)\) "sig": sigmoid-like saturation, \(f(x) = x/(x + K)\) Throughout, \(N_{,j}(t)\) denotes cumulative number events \(\) \(j\) (including) time \(t\); \(N_{,j}^w(t)\) windowed analogue \((t-w,\,t)\); \(d_i^{\mathrm{}}(t) = |\{l: N_{,l}(t)>0\}|\) historical -degree \(\); \(c_i^{\mathrm{}}(t) = \sum_l N_{,l}(t)\) total event count sent \(\). superscript \(\mathrm{act}\) indicates quantity computed currently active DEM network. implemented terms grouped five categories: Baseline Nuisance Terms: Intercept, time-varying     baseline, degree fixed effects. Endogenous Dyadic Terms: Inertia, reciprocity,     interaction duration, participation shifts. Triadic Closure Shared Partners: Common partners     triangle statistics. Degree Centrality Statistics: Actor degree     event count statistics. Exogenous Covariates: Dyadic monadic covariate terms.

title: redeem Model Terms — redeem_terms
text: K Numeric; evaluation point scaling/saturation factor sufficient statistic (default 1). transformation Character; specifies transformation apply statistic. One : "identity" (default): \(f(x) = x\) "log": \(f(x) = \log(x+1)\) "recip": \(f(x) = 1/(x+1)\) "bin": \(f(x) = (x>0)\) "sig": sigmoid-like saturation, \(f(x) = x/(x+K)\) event_stream Optional matrix data frame; alternative event stream use calculating statistic. NULL (default), modeled stream used. window Numeric; time window calculating statistic (default Inf, .e., use full history). type Character; specific variation statistic triangle type (e.g., "OSP", "ISP", "OTP", "ITP", "out_sender", "sum"). mode Character; participation shift mode (e.g., "ABBA", "ABBY"). data dyadic_cov, numeric matrix dimensions \(N \times N\), scalar applied globally, named list matrices time-varying covariates. monadic_cov, numeric vector length \(N\) named list vectors time-varying covariates. fun function taking two arguments fun(v_i, v_j) generate dyadic values. change_points Optional numeric vector; time points time-varying covariates data list. changepoints Numeric vector; time points baseline intensity allowed change. labels Character vector; optional labels resulting time intervals. history Character; "general" cumulative history "current" currently active events. count Logical; TRUE, uses count-based (weighted) versions degree statistics (default FALSE). ... Arguments passed underlying initialization function.

title: Relational Event Model (REM) Estimation — rem
text: Relational Event Model characterizes instantaneous rate actor pair \((,j)\) initiates event. log-linear specification, event intensity time \(t\) : $$\lambda_{,j}(t \mid \mathscr{H}_t,\, \theta) =   \exp\!\bigl(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)^\top \alpha +   \beta_i + \beta_j + f(t, \gamma)\bigr)$$ : \(s_{,j}(\mathscr{H}_t)\) vector sufficient statistics     computed event history \(\mathscr{H}_t\); see     redeem_terms available terms. \(\alpha\) vector covariate effects. \(\beta_i\) \(\beta_j\) optional actor-specific     baselines (sender receiver sociality), included via bare     symbol degree formula. \(f(t, \gamma)\) optional piecewise-constant temporal     baseline, included via baseline(changepoints) formula.

title: Relational Event Model (REM) Estimation — rem
text: semiparametric = TRUE, temporal baseline rate event occurrence left completely unspecified, model parameters estimated via Cox partial likelihood using survival package. path: observed event time treated failure time, non-occurring     dyads time constitute risk set. exact waiting times events conditioned away, meaning     inference based solely sequence events relative dyadic intensities. approach equivalent ordered (conditional)     REM likelihood introduced Butts (2008). highly robust temporal     fluctuations baseline misspecification since piecewise baseline changepoints     need specified. Limitations: path support specialized     scalable estimation sender/receiver popularity effects (degree)     piecewise-constant temporal baselines.
